---
title: "Net Neutrality and the Open Internet"
subtitle: "Internet Censorship and Online Speech"
author: "Nick Feamster · University of Chicago"
header-includes: |
  <style>
    /* No ALL-CAPS anywhere */
    .reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3, .reveal h4, .reveal h5, .reveal h6,
    .reveal .title, .reveal .subtitle,
    section.title-slide h1, section.title-slide .subtitle {
      text-transform: none !important;
      letter-spacing: normal !important;
    }
    /* Uniform title sizes (absolute px — unaffected by .smaller) */
    .reveal h1, .reveal .smaller h1, .reveal section.smaller h1 { font-size: 46px !important; }
    .reveal h2, .reveal .smaller h2, .reveal section.smaller h2 { font-size: 38px !important; }
    .reveal h3, .reveal .smaller h3, .reveal section.smaller h3 { font-size: 30px !important; }
    section.title-slide h1 { font-size: 56px !important; }
    section.title-slide .subtitle { font-size: 32px !important; }
    /* Body sizing (a bit bigger than before) */
    .reveal { font-size: 30px; }
    .reveal .smaller, .reveal section.smaller { font-size: 0.78em; }
    .reveal ul, .reveal ol, .reveal p, .reveal blockquote { font-size: 0.88em; }
    .reveal li { line-height: 1.3; margin-bottom: 0.15em; }
    .reveal table { font-size: 0.72em; }
    .reveal pre, .reveal code { font-size: 0.72em; }
  </style>
---

## The Network Should Not Pick Winners {.center}

> A neutral network carries every application's bits the same way — it does not
> **block**, **throttle**, or **sell prioritization** to favor one source over another.

The principle is simple. The regulation has been anything but — repealed,
reinstated, and struck down across three administrations.

::: {.notes}
Open with the principle, not the law. Net neutrality is the policy expression of
the end-to-end argument: the network is a dumb pipe; intelligence lives at the
edges. The whole lecture is about why such a simple idea has produced 15+ years of
regulatory whiplash, and how it connects to information control. See censorship-book
Ch. 4, §4.3.
:::

## Why This Belongs in a Censorship Course

An ISP sits on the **path between every user and everything they read**. If it can
discriminate among sources, it holds a **point of control**.

- **Blocking** a source is the bluntest form of information control
- **Throttling** is **friction** — a *tax on access* to disfavored content
- **Paid prioritization** lets the richest content buy reach; everyone else is
  relatively slowed

The same levers a regulator polices for *competition* are levers for **speech**.

::: {.notes}
Tie back to the course thesis (Roberts: Fear / Friction / Flooding). Throttling is
textbook friction — you don't have to ban a site, you just make it slow enough that
most people give up. Net neutrality rules are one of the few legal instruments that
directly constrain that friction. Keep the framing on *information control as a tax
on access*.
:::

# First, the Internet's Business Model {.center}

## How Networks Interconnect {.smaller}

The Internet is **tens of thousands of independently operated networks** (autonomous
systems) that interconnect. Two basic money relationships:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Customer–Provider**

One AS *pays* another for reachability to some set of destinations.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Settlement-Free Peering**

Two ASes *swap traffic for free* because both benefit — essentially bartering.
:::
:::

Net neutrality fights happen at these **interconnection points** — so we have to
keep the *economics* and the *speech* questions separate.

::: {.notes}
This is the foundation the book builds the whole section on (§4.3, Internet business
model figure). Most students think of "the Internet" as one network; correct that.
The key tension later: a congested *peering link* looks identical to *throttling*
from the user's seat, but one is an economics dispute and the other is information
control. Preferences are implemented with BGP local-preference manipulation.
:::

# The Regulatory History {.center}

## The 2015 Open Internet Order {.smaller}

In **March 2015** the FCC reclassified broadband ISPs as **Title II common
carriers** — regulating them like a public utility. The **bright-line rules**:

- **No blocking** of lawful content
- **No throttling** of lawful traffic
- **No paid prioritization** — no selling a "fast lane"
- **No unreasonable interference** or disadvantage; plus **transparency**

...with carve-outs for **"reasonable network management"** (security, congestion).

::: {.notes}
Title II is the hook everything hangs on: it is the statutory authority that lets the
FCC impose the bright-line rules. Note the rules don't ban *all* prioritization — an
ISP may prioritize all video over all bulk transfer. What's barred is *picking among
competitors*: FaceTime over Skype, or Netflix over a rival. That distinction matters
for the censorship angle.
:::

## A Microscope on ISPs — and a Blind Spot {.smaller}

The 2015 order trained a microscope on **ISPs**. But it left **most of the
ecosystem untouched**:

- **CDNs, clouds, search, social platforms** were *outside* the FCC's remit
- Discrimination, prioritization, and blocking remained **fair game** above the
  ISP layer

The order policed the *pipes* while the real consolidation was moving **up the
stack**.

::: {.notes}
This is the book's "barking up the wrong tree" point. The most acute modern threats
to open access — deplatforming, ranking, app-store removal — sit with a handful of
platforms and clouds the order never reached (Ch. 3). Foreshadow: even if NN rules
were airtight, they'd miss where information control actually concentrates today.
:::

## Repeal, Reinstate, Repeat {.smaller}

Net neutrality is a **political football** — the classification flips with the party
controlling the FCC:

| Year | Action |
|---|---|
| **2015** | FCC adopts Open Internet Order (Title II) |
| **2017** | FCC repeals it ("Restoring Internet Freedom") |
| **2024** | FCC reinstates Title II net neutrality |
| **2025** | A federal court strikes the reinstatement down |

::: {.notes}
The whiplash is the story. Each FCC majority "vacillates" (the Sixth Circuit's word).
The point for students: a rule that can be reversed every four years is a weak
foundation for a *right* — which is partly why advocates moved to legislation and to
the states. Set up the 2025 vignette next.
:::

## The Rules Are Struck Down (2025) {.smaller}

::: {.vignette}
On **January 2, 2025**, the **Sixth Circuit** in *Ohio Telecom Association v. FCC*
**vacated** the FCC's 2024 net neutrality order. Applying the Supreme Court's 2024
*Loper Bright* decision (which ended **Chevron deference**), the court read the
statute itself and held broadband is a lightly regulated **"information service,"**
not a Title II **"telecommunications service"** — so the FCC **lacks authority** to
impose the bright-line rules. The court said this "means we can end the FCC's
vacillations."
:::

Federal net neutrality is, for now, **dead** — but **state laws** (e.g.,
**California's 2018 Act**, upheld by the Ninth Circuit) survive.

::: {.notes}
This is the freshest verified hook — swap it as the law moves (see coverage-notes).
Two teaching points: (1) Post-Chevron, courts no longer defer to the FCC's
classification, so the regulatory ping-pong may finally stop — in the *deregulatory*
direction. (2) The same ruling weakens federal *preemption* of states, so California
and Washington's mirror laws keep ISPs largely in line nationwide anyway. Source:
6th Cir. opinion, Jan 2, 2025; CRS LSB11264.
:::

# The Hard Case: Congestion vs. Censorship {.center}

## "Was Comcast Blocking Netflix?" (2014) {.smaller}

In **2014**, users saw streaming video crawl at peak hours. The popular claim — even
**John Oliver** ran with it — was that ISPs were **blocking** Netflix.

The reality was **more complicated**: congestion can occur at *many* points along
the path —

- ISP ↔ transit-provider **interconnections**
- *inside* a transit provider's network
- transit ↔ content-provider links

::: {.notes}
The 2014 episode is the book's centerpiece for teaching the difference between a
*speech* problem and an *economics* problem. The accusation ("blocking") was the
language of censorship; the cause turned out to be an interconnection/peering
dispute. We're going to follow the measurement evidence and see it wasn't where
everyone assumed.
:::

## The Evidence: Congestion in Transit, Not the ISP {.smaller}

![Median download throughput across transit provider **Cogent** in NYC, by ISP (2013–2014). Throughput collapses, then jumps sharply once interconnects are upgraded.](images/slide011_img012.png)

Measurement Lab tests *appeared* to show ISP congestion — but the bottleneck was in a
**transit provider (Cogent)**, and the cross-AS test path made it hard to localize.

::: {.notes}
Walk the plot: sustained low throughput through 2013–early 2014, then a step-change
recovery once links were augmented. The subtlety the book stresses: the M-Lab test
traversed multiple ASes end-to-end, so it measured the *worst* hop, not the ISP. And
Cogent was itself prioritizing retail over wholesale traffic during congestion —
behavior easy to mistake for ISP throttling.
:::

## Two Sides of a Classic Peering Dispute {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Netflix's view**

By paying transit (Cogent), it *already paid* to reach Comcast's **"eyeballs."**
Comcast let the interconnects **congest** by not upgrading them.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Comcast's view**

Netflix can **congest any link at will** and **choose its transit** — so balancing
traffic across paths is *Netflix's* job.
:::
:::

Resolved in the **open market**: Netflix paid Comcast to **augment capacity**. The
market, arguably, **worked**.

::: {.notes}
This is the crux distinction the section wants students to leave with: a *peering /
interconnection dispute* is about **economics** (who pays to upgrade a link), not
about **blocking, throttling, or paid prioritization** in the speech sense. Related,
but distinct. The deal that settled it (Netflix paying Comcast directly) is exactly
the "paid interconnection" that confuses people into calling it a NN violation.
:::

## Why the 2014 Disputes Faded {.smaller}

Incentives **realigned**. Today an access ISP has every reason to **provision links
well** to the big content providers:

- ISP customers **depend on** Netflix, Amazon, Google, YouTube
- Much ISP **infrastructure and content** now lives on those same clouds
- An ISP whose customers **can't reach the sites they want has no business**

So peering disputes like 2014 are **largely dead for now** — even without
regulation.

::: {.notes}
The book's market-incentives argument. It's the strongest counter to "we need NN
rules to stop throttling": in the US, ISP behavior has actually been pretty good
*absent* federal rules, because the economics now favor cooperation. Push students:
is good behavior driven by incentives durable, or just contingent? Where could it
break? (No competition in last-mile; vertically integrated ISP-owned content.)
:::

# Bringing It Together {.center}

## The Many Faces of "Net Neutrality" {.smaller}

The phrase bundles **very different things** — only some are about speech:

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Information-control levers**

- No **blocking**
- No **throttling**
- No **paid prioritization**
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Economic / administrative**

- Title II **classification** itself
- Telecom **access fees**, USF
- **Outage reporting**, privacy rules
- Peering / interconnection **economics**
:::
:::

...all very different from the rules **Google, Meta, or app stores** operate under.

::: {.notes}
Disentangle the bundle. When people argue about "net neutrality" they're often
arguing about different things at once: the speech-relevant bright-line rules vs. the
public-utility regulatory apparatus vs. interconnection economics. For *this* course,
only the bright-line rules sit squarely in the information-control frame.
:::

## Where the Threat Actually Lives Now {.smaller}

The bright-line rules target the **ISP**. But the leverage to **block access to
large swaths of content** has moved **up the stack**:

- A few **cloud hosts, CDNs, search engines, app stores, and social platforms**
- These can make **one decision** that removes content for millions
- That's the **consolidation** story of Ch. 3 — and it's *outside* net neutrality

Net neutrality polices the **pipe**; modern information control sits at the
**platform**.

::: {.notes}
Land the synthesis. Net neutrality remains a useful *frame* — "the network shouldn't
pick winners among applications" — but as a *regulatory instrument* it aims at a layer
where, in the US, the acute threat has receded. The deplatforming / consolidation
risk the course cares about lives one layer up, where the bright-line rules don't
reach. This is the bridge back to the Platform chapter.
:::

## Summary {.smaller}

- Net neutrality = whether ISPs may **discriminate** among sources via **blocking,
  throttling, or paid prioritization** — each a soft form of **information control**
- US regulation **comes and goes**: 2015 → 2017 → 2024 → struck down **Jan 2025**;
  **state laws** persist
- The 2014 "Comcast vs. Netflix" fight was a **peering/economics dispute**, *not*
  blocking — keep the two **distinct**
- **Market incentives** now push ISPs toward openness; the sharper threats have moved
  **up the stack** to platforms and clouds

*See censorship-book Ch. 4 Legal and Economic Controls, §4.3 (Net Neutrality).*

::: {.notes}
The principle outlives the rules: "the network should not pick winners among
applications" is a durable analytical frame even when the formal regulation is not in
force. Next deck drills into the *mechanics* of paid prioritization specifically.
:::
